title: Cat or Kitten Fell from a High Surface: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Assess your cat's injuries after a fall from a height. Learn when to seek veterinary care and why visual monitoring is crucial for recovery.
content_html:
What happened?

When a cat or kitten falls from a high surface—sometimes called 'High-Rise Syndrome'—the impact can cause a variety of injuries. Even though cats are known for their ability to land on their feet, the force of the landing can lead to bone fractures, dental injuries, or internal trauma that may not be immediately visible on the skin.

Is it an emergency? (Urgency Level: Medium)

A fall is generally considered a Medium Urgency situation. While your cat may appear fine initially, adrenaline can mask pain or internal damage. You should seek veterinary care if you notice limping, hiding, changes in breathing, or a loss of appetite. If your cat is unconscious, bleeding from the mouth or nose, or unable to move, treat this as a high-priority emergency and head to a clinic immediately.

How a photo or video helps triage

Capturing a photo of your cat's current resting position or a short video of them attempting to walk can be vital for remote triage. These visuals allow a veterinary professional to assess their gait, posture, and respiratory effort. Subtle signs of discomfort, such as a hunched back or dilated pupils, are often easier to identify through high-quality images than through a text description alone.

faqs.question: How long should I monitor my cat after a fall?
faqs.answer: You should monitor your cat closely for at least 24 to 48 hours, as some internal injuries or symptoms of shock can take several hours to manifest.

faqs.question: What are the signs of internal injury in cats?
faqs.answer: Signs include pale gums, labored or rapid breathing, extreme lethargy, a distended abdomen, or coughing up blood.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for pain after a fall?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain medications to cats, as many are highly toxic. Always consult a veterinarian for cat-safe pain management.
